It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - **Board**: 64×64 grid with charcoal top bar (rows 0-9), black main area (rows 10-62), white bottom bar (row 63, likely HUD). - **Blue agent**: ~8×9 blue blob at upper-right (rows 11-19, cols 44-52) containing a dark gray 3×3 core. - **Diagonal trail**: 20 dark gray (G) single pixels forming a diagonal line from near the blue agent (19,44) down to (57,6). - **White cross**: 5-pixel plus shape at (52-54, 9-11), near bottom-left. - **Purple squares**: Two 3×3 purple blocks — one in header (4-6, 30-32), one at bottom-left (58-60, 3-5). - **Goal hypothesis**: Move the blue agent along the diagonal trail toward the white cross, or move the cross to the blue agent's position. Purple squares may mark start/end markers.
- Goal model: Navigate the blue agent along the diagonal path to the cross, or align the cross with the purple marker.
- Action model: MOUSE likely clicks/interacts with objects; ACTION7 unknown.
- Plan: Probe by clicking on the white cross first to see what happens.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me understand what happened:
1. The white cross at (52-54, 9-11) turned into a purple 3×3 square (same hash as the other purple squares: 613f4a27ea77da2d)
2. The purple square at (58-60, 3-5) disappeared and the bottom bar changed
3. The diagonal trail is still intact (20 G dots)
4. The blue agent is still in place
So clicking on the cross transformed it into a purple square. The purple square at bottom-left seems to have been absorbed into the HUD bar. The bottom bar now has "WW...WWBB" instead of all W.
The pattern seems to be: clicking on objects along the diagonal path transforms them. The cross was the first non-trail object near the bottom of the trail. Now it's a purple square matching the others.
Looking at the diagonal trail, it goes from the blue agent area down to (57,6). The purple square is now at (52-54, 9-11). The trail dots continue past it to (55,8) and (57,6).
I think the goal might be to click on all the trail dots in order, or to click on objects to move something along the path. Let me try clicking on one of the trail dots to see what happens.
Actually, wait - looking more carefully at the image, I see the purple square is now where the cross was. The trail continues past it. Maybe I need to click on the trail dots to "activate" them, or click on the blue agent to move it along the trail.
Let me try clicking on the blue agent to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (revised):
- **Clicking the white cross** transformed it into a purple 3×3 square (same hash as other purple markers). The old purple square at bottom-left was absorbed into the HUD bar.
- **Diagonal trail**: Still 20 dark gray dots from (19,44) to (57,6), unchanged.
- **Blue agent**: Still at upper-right, unchanged.
- **Goal hypothesis**: Need to interact with objects along the diagonal path. The cross→purple transformation suggests clicking "activates" or "moves" objects. May need to click the blue agent to move it along the trail.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the blue agent center to see if it moves along the trail
# Blue agent is at rows 11-19, cols 44-52, center roughly (15, 48)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 15, 'col': 48}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
